perturbation.original_text: 4.1.1 This Agreement shall remain in effect for a term of [number of months or years ]. Notwithstanding the foregoi ng, the Receiving Party’s duty to hold in confidence Conf i- dential Information that was disclosed during the term shal l remain in effect indefinitely, save otherwise agreed.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1.1 This Agreement shall remain in effect for a term of [number of months or years ]. Notwithstanding the foregoi ng, the Receiving Party’s duty to hold in confidence Conf i- dential Information that was disclosed during the term shal l cease after one (1) year from the termination of this agreement, save otherwise agreed.
perturbation.explanation: The modification limits the confidentiality obligation to one year, potentially conflicting with trade secret laws that often require indefinite protection for genuinely confidential information. Many jurisdictions recognize that trade secrets can maintain their protected status indefinitely as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage.
perturbation.law_explanation: UTSA protects trade secrets indefinitely as long as they remain confidential and provide economic value. Limiting confidentiality to one year contradicts the indefinite protection required for trade secrets under UTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The original contract provides indefinite confidentiality, while the changed contract limits it to one year. The Perturbation Explanation states that this contradicts trade secret laws which often require indefinite protection. The Law Explanation reinforces this by stating that UTSA protects trade secrets indefinitely as long as they remain confidential and provide economic value. The altered contract's one-year limit directly undermines this indefinite protection, creating a legal contradiction because it fails to comply with the potentially indefinite protection required by trade secret laws. Although Snippet 1 is missing, and Snippet 2 is a 404 error, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation sufficiently indicate a contradiction based on established trade secret law principles.

perturbation.original_text: 3.7 The Receiving Party shall promptly return or destroy all copies (in whatever form reproduced or stored) , including all notes and derivatives of the Confidential Information disclosed un der this Agreement, upon the earlier of (i) the completion or termination of the dealings contemplated in this Agreement; (ii) or the termination of this Agreement; (iii) or at the time the Disclosing Party may r equest it to the Receiving Party .
perturbation.changed_text: 3.7 The Receiving Party may retain all copies (in whatever form reproduced or stored) , including all notes and derivatives of the Confidential Information disclosed un der this Agreement, even after (i) the completion or termination of the dealings contemplated in this Agreement; (ii) or the termination of this Agreement; (iii) or at the time the Disclosing Party may r equest it to the Receiving Party .
perturbation.explanation: This change allows the Receiving Party to retain confidential information indefinitely, even after the agreement terminates or the Disclosing Party requests its return. This contradicts legal requirements for data protection and trade secret preservation, which often necessitate the return or destruction of confidential materials upon termination of an agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 17 of the GDPR grants individuals the 'right to be forgotten,' requiring data controllers to erase personal data when it is no longer necessary for the purpose it was collected. Allowing indefinite retention of confidential information, especially if it contains personal data, violates this right.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Art. 17 GDPR – Right to erasure (‘right to be forgotten’) - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller the erasure of personal data concerning him or her without undue delay and the controller shall have the obligation to erase personal data without undue delay where one of the following grounds applies: the personal data are no longer necessary in relation to the purposes for which they were collected or otherwise processed; the data subject withdraws consent on which the processing is based according to point (a) of Article 6 (1), or point (a) of Article 9 (2), and where there is no other legal ground for the processing; the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21 (1) and there are no overriding legitimate grounds for the processing, or the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21 (2); the personal data have been unlawfully processed; the personal data have to be erased for compliance with a legal obligation in Union or Member State law to which the controller is subject; the personal data have been collected in relation to the offer of information society services referred to in Article 8 (1). Where the controller has made the personal data public and is obliged pursuant to paragraph 1 to erase the personal data, the controller, taking account of available technology and the cost of implementation, shall take reasonable steps, including technical measures, to inform controllers which are processing the personal data that the data subject has requested the erasure by such controllers of any links to, or copy or replication of, those personal data. Paragraphs 1 and 2 shall not apply to the extent that processing is necessary: for exercising the right of freedom of expression and information; for compliance with a legal obligation which requires processing by Union or Member State law to w
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the 'right to be forgotten' as outlined in Article 17 of the GDPR, detailed in Scraped Law Snippet 1. The original contract mandates the return or destruction of confidential information upon specific events, aligning with data protection principles. However, the modified contract permits the Receiving Party to retain this information indefinitely, even after the agreement's termination or upon request by the Disclosing Party. This directly opposes the GDPR's requirement for data controllers to erase personal data when it is no longer necessary or when consent is withdrawn, leading to a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 4.2 Applicable Law and Jurisdiction This Agreement shall be construed and interpreted by the laws of [choose the applicable law]. The court of [choose the jurisdiction to settle disputes ] shall have jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.2 Applicable Law and Jurisdiction This Agreement shall be construed and interpreted by the laws of [choose the applicable law]. Any disputes arising under this agreement shall be resolved through binding arbitration in a jurisdiction of the Receiving Party's choosing, waiving all rights to a court trial.
perturbation.explanation: Forcing arbitration in the Receiving Party's chosen jurisdiction and waiving the right to a court trial can be seen as unconscionable and may be unenforceable, especially if the chosen jurisdiction has little connection to the agreement or the Disclosing Party. This can violate principles of fair and impartial dispute resolution.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the FAA generally favors arbitration, courts can refuse to enforce arbitration agreements that are unconscionable or obtained through fraud, duress, or undue influence. A clause forcing arbitration in a distant, inconvenient jurisdiction chosen solely by one party could be deemed unconscionable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate U.S. Code Notes prev | next A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising out of such a contract, transaction, or refusal, shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract or as otherwise provided in chapter 4. (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 670 ; Pub. L. 117–90, § 2(b)(1)(A) , Mar. 3, 2022 , 136 Stat. 27 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 2, 43 Stat. 883 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2022— Pub. L. 117–90 inserted “or as otherwise provided in chapter 4” before period at end. Statutory Notes and Related Subsidiaries Effective Date of 2022 Amendment Amendment by Pub. L. 117–90 applicable with respect to any dispute or claim that arises or accrues on or after Mar. 3, 2022 , see section 3 of Pub. L. 117–90 , set out as an Effective Date note under section 401 of this title . U.S. Code Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities How current is this?
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a clause forcing arbitration in a jurisdiction chosen by the Receiving Party, waiving all rights to a court trial. This contradicts the principle of fair and impartial dispute resolution. While 9 U.S. Code § 2 generally validates arbitration agreements, it also states that such agreements are enforceable "save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract." The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest that a clause forcing arbitration in a distant, inconvenient jurisdiction chosen solely by one party could be deemed unconscionable, which constitutes grounds for revocation under law or equity as contemplated in 9 U.S. Code § 2. Therefore, the modified clause creates a legally questionable situation and possibly an unenforceable contract. The change does not necessarily void the contract but introduces a strong basis for legal challenge based on fairness and unconscionability.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1 In the context of discussi ons, preparations or negotiations, the Disclosing Party may disclose Confidential Information to the Receiving Party. The Receiving Party agrees to use the Confidential Information solely in connection with purposes contemplated b e- tween the Parties in this Agreement and not to use it for any other purpose or without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1 In the context of discussi ons, preparations or negotiations, the Disclosing Party may disclose Confidential Information to the Receiving Party. The Receiving Party agrees to use the Confidential Information for any purpose it deems necessary or beneficial, without needing the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: This change allows the Receiving Party to use the Confidential Information for any purpose, even purposes that are detrimental to the Disclosing Party, contradicting the fundamental purpose of a non-disclosure agreement. It negates the obligation to protect and limit the use of confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: Although not a statute, Restatement (Second) of Torts § 757 is widely adopted and defines trade secret misappropriation to occur when one discloses or uses another's trade secret without privilege to do so. The modified term here removes all restrictions on use, directly contradicting trade secret law and basic principles of confidentiality.
perturbation.justification: The original text requires the Receiving Party to use Confidential Information solely for contemplated purposes and with the Disclosing Party's consent. The changed text allows the Receiving Party to use the Confidential Information for any purpose they deem necessary, without prior consent. The Perturbation Explanation clarifies this negates the purpose of a non-disclosure agreement. The Law Explanation notes this contradicts trade secret law regarding unauthorized use. Since the changed text directly removes restrictions on use and allows for any purpose, it contradicts the principle of protecting confidential information, which underlies trade secret law, even without specific law snippets.

perturbation.original_text: 3.3 The Receiving Party will use the Confidential Information exclusively for the permi t- ted purpose stated in claus e 2 and not use the information for its own purposes or ben e- fit.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.3 The Receiving Party will have no obligation to ensure the Confidential Information's security and will not be liable for any security breaches or unauthorized disclosures.
perturbation.explanation: This modification eliminates the Receiving Party's responsibility to protect the Confidential Information and absolves them of liability for breaches. This is contrary to the implied duty of care in confidentiality agreements and laws related to data protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: While focused on consumer data, the CCPA establishes a standard of reasonable security for personal information. A clause that explicitly removes any obligation to ensure the security of confidential information, potentially including personal information, conflicts with the CCPA's general requirement for reasonable security measures.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) | State of California - Department of Justice - Office of the Attorney General Skip to main content California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Home Privacy California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Updated on March 13, 2024 The California Consumer Privacy Act of 2018 (CCPA) gives consumers more control over the personal information that businesses collect about them and the CCPA regulations provide guidance on how to implement the law. This landmark law secures new privacy rights for California consumers, including: The right to know about the personal information a business collects about them and how it is used and shared; The right to delete personal information collected from them (with some exceptions); The right to opt-out of the sale or sharing of their personal information including via the GPC ; The right to non-discrimination for exercising their CCPA rights. In November of 2020, California voters approved Proposition 24, the CPRA , which amended the CCPA and added new additional privacy protections that began on January 1, 2023. As of January 1, 2023, consumers have new rights in addition to those above, such as: The right to correct inaccurate personal information that a business has about them; and The right to limit the use and disclosure of sensitive personal information collected about them. Businesses that are subject to the CCPA have several responsibilities, including responding to consumer requests to exercise these rights and giving consumers certain notices explaining their privacy practices . The CCPA applies to many businesses, including data brokers . CPRA amends the CCPA; it does not create a separate, new law. As a result, our office typically refers to the law as “CCPA” or “CCPA, as amended.” Links to Topics A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA B. RIGHT TO OPT-OUT OF SALE OR SHARING C. REQUESTS TO KNOW D. REQUESTS TO DELETE E. REQUESTS TO CORRECT F. REQUESTS TO LIMIT USE OF PERSONAL INFORMATION G. RIGHT TO NON-DISCRIMINATION H. REQUIRED NOTICES I. DATA BROKERS AND THE CCPA Other Consumer Resources on CCPA Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs) These FAQs provide general consumer information about the CCPA and how you can exercise your rights under the CCPA. They are not legal advice, regulatory guidance, or an opinion of the Attorney General. We will update this information periodically. A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA 1. What rights do I have under the CCPA? If you are a California resident, you may ask businesses to disclose what personal information they have about you and what they do with that information, to delete your personal information, to direct businesses not to sell or share your personal information, to correct inaccurate information that they have about you, and to limit businesses’ use and disclosure of your sensitive personal information: Right to know : You can request that a business disclose to you: (1) the categories and/or specific pieces of personal information they have collected about you, (2) the categories of sources for that personal information, (3) the purposes for which the business uses that information, (4) the categories of third parties with whom the business discloses the information, and (5) the categories of information that the business sells or discloses to third parties. You can make a request to know up to twice a year, free of charge. Right to delete : You can request that businesses delete personal information they collected from you and tell their service providers to do the same, subject to certain exceptions (such as if the business is legally required to keep the information). Right to opt-out of sale or sharing : You may request that businesses stop selling or sharing your personal information (“opt-out”), including via a user-enabled global privacy control. Businesses cannot sell or share your personal information after they receive your opt-out request unless you later authorize them to do so again. Right to correc
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the Receiving Party's responsibility to protect confidential information and absolves them of liability for breaches, which directly contradicts the CCPA's requirement for reasonable security measures to protect personal information. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, 'Any consumer whose nonencrypted and nonredacted personal information...is subject to an unauthorized access and exfiltration, theft, or disclosure as a result of the business’ violation of the duty to implement and maintain reasonable security procedures and practices...may institute a civil action'. The changed text eliminates this duty entirely, creating a clear legal contradiction by negating the obligation to maintain reasonable security.
